BSTD
A lightweight C library providing common data structures and utilities for systems programming.

Dynamic Strings

Heap allocated string utilities. More...

file  string.h
 Dynamic string implementation.

 Removes all characters from a string.

Detailed Description

Heap allocated string utilities.

Macro Definition Documentation

◆ BSTD_STRING_NULL_TERM

#define BSTD_STRING_NULL_TERM   1

Function Documentation

◆ bstd_string_clear()

void bstd_string_clear ( bstd_string *  s)

Removes all characters from a string.

Sets the string length to zero and writes a null terminator at the beginning of the buffer.

The allocated capacity remains unchanged.

Parameters
sString to clear.

◆ bstd_string_clone()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_clone ( bstd_string *  s)

Creates a deep copy of a string.

Allocates a new buffer and copies the contents of the source string.

The returned string owns its memory independently from the original string.

Parameters
sString to clone.
Returns
A result containing the cloned string on success, or an error if allocation fails.

◆ bstd_string_concat()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_concat ( bstd_string  s,
bstd_string  d 
)

Concatenates two strings.

Creates a new string containing the contents of s followed by the contents of d.

The returned string owns its memory independently from both source strings.

Parameters
sLeft-hand string.
dRight-hand string.
Returns
A result containing the concatenated string on success, or an error if allocation fails.

◆ bstd_string_concat_inplace()

bstd_result bstd_string_concat_inplace ( bstd_string *  s,
bstd_string  d 
)

Appends one string to another.

Extends s by appending the contents of d.

The internal buffer may be reallocated if additional capacity is required.

Parameters
sDestination string.
dString to append.
Returns
A success result or an error if allocation fails.

◆ bstd_string_destroy()

void bstd_string_destroy ( bstd_string *  s)

Releases the memory owned by a string.

Frees the internal buffer and resets all fields to their default values.

Parameters
sString to destroy.
Warning
The string must not be used after calling this function unless it is reinitialized.

◆ bstd_string_new()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_new ( char *  cstr)

Creates a new dynamic string.

Allocates memory on the heap and copies the contents of the provided C string.

Parameters
cstrNull-terminated string to copy.
Returns
A result containing the created string or an error if allocation failed.

◆ bstd_string_push_char()

bstd_result bstd_string_push_char ( bstd_string *  s,
char  c 
)

Appends a character to the end of a string.

Increases the string length by one and places the specified character at the end.

The internal buffer may be reallocated if additional capacity is required.

Parameters
sString to modify.
cCharacter to append.
Returns
A success result or an error if allocation fails.

◆ bstd_string_replace()

bstd_result bstd_string_replace ( bstd_string *  s,
const char *  search,
const char *  replace 
)

Replaces the first occurrence of a substring.

Searches for the first occurrence of search within the string and replaces it with replace.

The internal buffer may be reallocated if the replacement requires additional capacity.

If the search string is not found, the string remains unchanged.

Parameters
sString to modify.
searchSubstring to search for.
replaceReplacement substring.
Returns
A success result, or an error if the input is invalid or memory allocation fails.
Warning
search must not be an empty string.

◆ bstd_string_reserve()

bstd_result bstd_string_reserve ( bstd_string *  s,
size_t  n 
)

Ensures a minimum capacity.

Reallocates the internal buffer if necessary so that the string can hold at least n characters without further allocations.

The string contents are preserved.

Parameters
sString to reserve capacity for.
nDesired minimum capacity.
Returns
A success result or an out-of-memory error.

◆ bstd_string_reverse()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_reverse ( bstd_string  s)

Creates a reversed copy of a string.

Allocates a new string containing the characters of the source string in reverse order.

The returned string owns its memory independently from the source string.

Parameters
sSource string.
Returns
A result containing the reversed string on success, or an error if allocation fails.

◆ bstd_string_shrink_to_fit()

bstd_result bstd_string_shrink_to_fit ( bstd_string *  s)

Shrinks the allocation to fit the current size.

Reallocates the internal buffer so that the capacity matches the current string length.

The string contents are preserved.

Parameters
sString to shrink.
Returns
A success result or an out-of-memory error.

◆ bstd_string_slice()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_slice ( const bstd_string *  s,
size_t  start_idx,
size_t  end_idx 
)

Creates a substring from a string.

Allocates memory on the heap and copies the characters in the range [start_idx, end_idx) into a new bstd_string.

The returned string owns its memory independently from the source string and must be destroyed when no longer needed.

Example:

bstd_string_result hello =
bstd_string_new("Hello World");
bstd_string_result slice =
bstd_string_slice(
&hello.value,
0,
5
);
// slice.value.data == "Hello"
bstd_string_new
bstd_string_result bstd_string_new(char *cstr)
Creates a new dynamic string.
Definition string.c:10
bstd_string_slice
bstd_string_result bstd_string_slice(const bstd_string *s, size_t start_idx, size_t end_idx)
Creates a substring from a string.
Definition string.c:32
bstd_string_result
Definition string.h:30
bstd_string_result::value
bstd_string value
Definition string.h:34
Parameters
sSource string.
start_idxStarting index (inclusive).
end_idxEnding index (exclusive).
Returns
A result containing the created substring on success, or an error if the indices are invalid or memory allocation fails.
Warning
The range must satisfy: start_idx <= end_idx <= s->length

◆ bstd_string_to_lowercase()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_to_lowercase ( const bstd_string *  s)

Converts a string to lowercase.

Creates a new string with all ASCII uppercase letters converted to their lowercase equivalents.

The returned string owns its memory independently from the source string and must be destroyed when no longer needed.

Parameters
sSource string.
Returns
A result containing the lowercase string on success, or an error if the input is invalid or memory allocation fails.
Warning
Only ASCII characters are transformed. UTF-8 and locale-specific characters are not supported.

◆ bstd_string_to_lowercase_inplace()

bstd_result bstd_string_to_lowercase_inplace ( bstd_string *  s)

Converts a string to lowercase in place.

Replaces all ASCII uppercase letters in the string with their lowercase equivalents.

No additional memory allocation is performed.

Parameters
sString to modify.
Returns
A success result or an error if the input string is invalid.
Warning
Only ASCII characters are transformed.

◆ bstd_string_to_uppercase()

bstd_string_result bstd_string_to_uppercase ( const bstd_string *  s)

Converts a string to uppercase.

Creates a new string with all ASCII lowercase letters converted to their uppercase equivalents.

The returned string owns its memory independently from the source string and must be destroyed when no longer needed.

Parameters
sSource string.
Returns
A result containing the uppercase string on success, or an error if the input is invalid or memory allocation fails.
Warning
Only ASCII characters are transformed. UTF-8 and locale-specific characters are not supported.

◆ bstd_string_to_uppercase_inplace()

bstd_result bstd_string_to_uppercase_inplace ( bstd_string *  s)

Converts a string to uppercase in place.

Replaces all ASCII lowercase letters in the string with their uppercase equivalents.

No additional memory allocation is performed.

Parameters
sString to modify.
Returns
A success result or an error if the input string is invalid.
Warning
Only ASCII characters are transformed.
Generated by  1.9.8
